data.title: RCM Technologies Q3 Preview: Grossly Undervalued, Even Under Conservative Estimates
data.description:
Introduction

RCM Technologies (NASDAQ:RCMT) is going to report Q3 ’24 earnings on November 6th, so I wanted to see what we should expect, give some comments on the company, and provide a valuation update. The company has been performing decently operationally speaking, while its share price has dropped by around 25% since the last time I covered it, which means the company is even more attractive than before; therefore, a strong buy rating is maintained. I will be adding more shares over the coming weeks, especially once Q3 numbers come out and if we don’t see anything too bad.

What to Expect from Q3

Analysts expect the company to earn $0.44 and $0.38 on an adjusted and GAAP basis, respectively, on $60.45m in sales. That is about 4% y/y growth in sales and -12% sequentially. So, analysts aren’t expecting a good quarter by the looks of it. Very little growth in the top line is expected, and a decrease in EPS. I wonder where these analysts, although there are only two that cover the company, got such low EPS estimates since the management is not guiding for Q3 at all. How likely is it the company will miss these expectations? Well, looking at the historical figures, in five out of the last eleven quarters, the company missed revenue estimates, some by quite a decent margin, while it only missed once on the EPS.

In my opinion, I expect the company to beat these, what I consider rather conservative, next quarter estimates. I would be very surprised if things go the wrong direction, and we see another plummet in share price.

What I would like to see

The company is slowly but surely getting away from that period of exuberance. The growth during the years of COVID-19 has been extraordinary and not comparable to today’s performance. If we strip out the effects of COVID-19, the company has been growing rather well. As per management comments in the Q2 earnings call, the company saw growth of around 19% in Healthcare and 23% in School revenue.

I would like to see this momentum continuing going forward and would like to see how the company’s school revenues are going to perform, which should be the busiest time of the year since the school year starts in September. The company is well on its way to adding another five school districts that bring in over $500k in revenues. The management seems rather optimistic that they will succeed in this mission; however, it is not given, so I will be looking out for some sort of clue in how that has developed and where that number is headed in the long run.

Gross margins have improved y/y, and I would like to see this continue in the next quarter, however, if we look at the previous quarter, there was an outsized growth in SG&A as compared to revenue growth, which meant that the improvement in gross margins didn’t trickle down to the bottom line at all. SG&A grew around 6% as compared to revenue growth of around 3%. Now, I would like to hear what the company has in store over the next few quarters to try and tackle the discrepancy, and with additional offices opening in lower-cost-of-labor countries like the Philippines, I would expect margins to continue to improve, but only time will tell.

Looking at the Engineering segment, we can see there was decent growth in Fixed Fees compared to last year, driven by growth in Energy Services, which is growing through some sort of a growth phase. I would like to see this carried forward and hope that the company takes advantage of the overall growth in the segment. Last quarter, the growth that the Engineering segment saw was around 24% y/y, so I would like to see similar numbers in Q3.

In the past, a lot of investors took an issue with the company’s receivables. It seems that the company has been slow at collecting the money that they are owed. It looks like it has been trending down the last quarter, so I would like to see this trend continue going forward.

Another item that I would like to see continue to come down is the company’s debt pile. It has increased 4-fold since the last time I covered the company, but in the last six months, the company has paid down $7m of the debt outstanding, so it is getting much more manageable. The company’s interest expense has been quite large since the debt it took on was floating and averaged at around 6.8%; however, now that the company has paid some of it down and that the interest rates have seen their first cut, it should be a lot easier to manage going forward.

Let’s look at an updated valuation.

Valuation

I’m going to keep it conservative as usual. For revenues, I went with a 5% CAGR over the next decade, which is much slower growth than its 3-year CAGR up to ’23, which was 13.6%.

For margins, the company is confident in improving them, however, to keep it conservative, I went with the margin profile that is forecasted for FY24, which means the company is not going to improve any further, and that’s a conservative take.

The company’s WACC is around 6.5%, but I thought it was a little low and not conservative enough; therefore, I went with a 10% discount rate to really keep these assumptions down. Additionally, I went with a 10% discount on the final intrinsic value calculation. With that said, RCMT’s intrinsic value is around $31 a share, which means it is trading at a decent discount to its (conservative) fair value.

Risks

Weakness in certain segments like Life Sciences and IT may keep the company’s share price down.

The company is facing a lot of competition in the segments it operates, which means they need to keep on top of the game to grow.

Speaking of growth, the low top-line performance is not appealing to many investors, which means it needs to step up its game to attract more investors and coverage.

The company needs to maintain the gross margin improvements going forward and lower operating expenses considerably so that all that margin improvement trickles down to the bottom line.

Closing Comment

Nothing has changed my outlook on the company in the last seven months since I covered it. Even with quite conservative estimates (in my view), the company is grossly undervalued; therefore, I am maintaining a strong buy rating.

I have been adding to my position over the last while and will continue to add slowly to get my average price down even further. So far, it is still a rather small position, and I am waiting to see what kind of numbers the company will put up in the next quarter and what the management has to say about next year if anything.

data.title: Yelp Stock: 3 Reasons We've Turned Bearish (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
We last covered Yelp Inc. (NYSE:YELP) in 2022 with a bullish outlook citing improving earnings at the time, in what, we believed, would be a banner year for the online crowdsourced business review and directory platform.

A lot has changed over the period. It turned out that 2023 was Yelp's breakout year, which saw shares climb to a 5-year high before reversing those gains. The stock is currently down around 5% from our original call and also 31% lower from its 52-week high.

Our update today downgrades the stock with a more bearish view. As we see it, despite steady growth and consistent profitability, several core operating metrics stand out as concerning. We believe the company's long-term outlook and growth potential have deteriorated, with risks tilted for further downside. Here are 4 reasons we recommend avoiding Yelp stock.

1) Poor Brand Momentum

A major theme for Yelp has been a shift in its advertising mix, away from restaurants, retail & other (RR&O) towards the services category that covers small businesses like home repair, plumbers, electricians, and beauty salons.

The trend has had a favorable impact on total revenue which reached $341 million in the last reported second quarter, up 6% year-over-year, driven precisely by a 15% increase in services. On the other hand, the decline in revenues from the RR&O category is noticeable, down -3% year-over-year and sequentially over the past three quarters.

In our view, the problem is that Yelp is losing its total number of paying advertisers by location as a proxy for "customers". The 531k total at the end of Q2 is down -6% y/y with the 7% increase in the number of service providers on the platform outweighed by the -15% y/y drop in RR&O locations. Moreover, the total number of paying advertised locations has been flat, going back to pre-pandemic levels.

The concern here is that Yelp is becoming less relevant in its traditional core market of restaurant reviews, which has important implications for site traffic and even the future potential in the service side of the business. If RR&O-type paying businesses are systematically leaving the platform, the risk is that the trend accelerates as remaining businesses begin to see lower user engagement and conversion.

There is also a question of how much more upside there is long-term with services, where despite Yelp managing to generate a higher average revenue per location, the number of providers is relatively constrained by city and region.

While the company doesn't share user data, we can extrapolate its ad clicks metric where even with a 9% y/y increase this past quarter, with Yelp pointing to improvements in its algorithm, the total number of ad clicks is approximately flat from levels in early 2022. In the company's Q2 shareholder letter, management anticipated the "year-over-year changes in ad clicks and average CPC since the second half of 2023 will moderate" due to more difficult prior-year comparisons.

2) Lower Quality Earnings

The other side of those soft operating metrics is the company's effort to improve profitability, with some measure of success. Yelp's $91 million in adjusted EBITDA for Q2 was 9% higher than last year with a 26% margin, up from 25% in Q2 2023.

That being said, the main factor contributing to that uptick was a decline in product development (PD) and general & administrative (G&A) costs as management moved to generate some financial efficiencies. These steps are commendable and show some initiative, but are not necessarily sustainable as a profitable growth strategy long term, particularly if the platform is struggling to retain paying advertisers.

For the full year, Yelp expects net revenue of around $1.42 billion, marking a 6% increase from 2023. The adjusted EBITDA full-year estimate at a $330 million midpoint estimate is less impressive, just flat for 2023.

The big question and uncertainty for Yelp will be how its RR&O business evolves and whether it can keep the Services ecosystem robust against a highly competitive landscape that includes specialized players like Angi Inc. (ANGI) or OpenTable as a subsidiary of Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG).

According to consensus, Yelp is forecast to generate mid-single-digit annual revenue growth through 2026 while EPS faces the headwind of ongoing CAPEX and development costs.

3) Yelp Could Be A Value-Trap

Ultimately, Yelp's main competition is from larger tech players including Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) which can leverage social media presence and search engines as an alternative for consumers to seek information and reviews, also representing an important advertising channel for all types of businesses.

Ultimately, it's very hard for Yelp to compete with sites like Facebook.com, Instagram, and Google Search for businesses to reach potential customers. We can add that advancements in artificial intelligence, where people can ask a chatbot directly for a restaurant recommendation, have further diminished Yelp's visibility which isn't the authoritative source of reviews it may have been 10-15 years ago.

Some investors may look at Yelp trading at just 10 times its full-year consensus EPS, or an EV to forward EBITDA ratio of 6x as a relative bargain next to these same tech giants. In our view, this deep discount is more than justified given Yelp's poor operating trends and the number of long-term uncertainties.

A Bearish Take On Yelp

We rate YELP as a sell with an expectation the ongoing share price selloff will continue. The risk for the stock is that further weakness in the RR&O advertising category, coupled with weaker-than-expected trends in the services market side of the operation, would lead to revisions to lower the company's earnings outlook.

At the same time, we'll be the first to come back here and admit we got the narrative wrong if Yelp manages to drive higher its total number of advertising locations and generate sharply stronger operating margins as key monitoring points. The stock could rally on evidence that revenues are accelerating as a tailwind for earnings.

data.title: iRhythm Technologies: Today's 510(K) Victory May Only Be A Temporary Reprieve
data.description:
Investment Overview

iRhythm (NASDAQ:IRTC) shareholders will be celebrating today after the San Francisco-based company announced this morning that the:

U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted clearance for its 510(k) submission related to prior design changes made to the Zio AT device via letter to file

To explain, back in May 2023, as reported in iRhythm's Q2 2024 quarterly report/10-Q submission:

The FDA raised questions in the warning letter issued on May 25, 2023 regarding certain changes and modifications to the Zio AT System for which we did not make 510(k) submissions, and rather documented our analysis in letters to file.

According to iRhythm's 2023 annual report/10-K submission:

The Zio Systems and Zio Services deliver a patient-friendly design that enables between 98%-99% patient compliance with minimal ECG data noise or artifact, thereby potentially delivering superior clinical accuracy to physicians diagnosing arrhythmias and reducing the cost of care for healthcare systems by avoiding costly downstream adverse events.

We currently offer three Zio System options — the Zio Monitor System, the Zio XT System, and the Zio AT System.

The Zio AT System is a prescription-only, remote ECG monitoring system that similarly consists of a patch ECG monitor (the “Zio AT patch") that records the electric signal from the heart continuously for up to 14 days and the ZEUS System, but which also incorporates the Zio AT wireless gateway that provides connectivity between the Zio AT patch and the ZEUS System during the patient wear period.

The wireless gateway, slightly larger than a smartphone, is provided to the patient at the time of Zio AT patch application and collects and transmits data from the Zio AT patch to the cloud via a long-term evolution [LTE] cellular protocol.

iRhythm does not break out sales of its three different Zio devices, so far as I can tell. However, in 2023, the company generated revenues of $493m, compared to $411m in 2022, earned gross profit of $331m, compared to $282m in the prior year, and made a net loss of $(123.4m), versus $(116m) in 2022.

Since receiving the FDA warning letter in May last year, iRhythm stock has endured a long bear run, falling in value from ~$128 per share, to a low of ~$57 per share (down 55%) but today's news has resulted in shares gaining >20% in value, to trade at $76 per share (at the time of writing), their highest value since the end of June.

Analysis - A Welcome Relief, But More Work To be Done

iRhythm's business - being a medical device company involved in remote cardiac monitoring - is dependent upon its products being extremely safe, reliable, and effective, and of course, compliant with the regulatory authorities.

iRhythm first gained clearance from the FDA for its technology in 2009. In its latest 10Q the company writes:

As permitted by applicable law, FDA allows device manufacturers to internally analyze and document a decision that a new clearance or approval is viewed by the manufacturer as unnecessary. Accordingly, we have made certain changes and modifications to our Zio Systems in the past that we believe did not require additional clearances or approvals by FDA.

Such internal decisions are, however, subject to review by FDA, and may require additional action in the event FDA questions earlier internal decision-making.

Essentially, iRhythm felt the changes it made did not require FDA approval, but the agency disagreed. Today's announcement will be a relief to the company and its shareholders, but there is still a second 510(k) submission requiring approval, which management says it expects to hear about "in the near future".

On its Q2 earnings call with analysts, iRhythm CEO Quentin Blackford told analysts:

Recall that we submitted two 510(k) files in January of this year. One is a catch-up for changes previously made to the Zio AT system as a letter to file and a second 510(k) for design features and labeling updates to further address areas of focus noted in the FDA warning letter.

The CEO went on to highlight further issues with the FDA as follows:

With respect to the FDA, the agency was on-site at our San Francisco and Orange County facilities in the back half of July. The inspections concluded yesterday with several 483 observations noted. We are in the early stages of evaluating these and we intend to provide responses to the FDA in a timely fashion.

At a high level, the observations were primarily focused on our quality system and regulatory compliance, including complaint handling and medical device reporting, risk analysis regarding the involvement of the company's certified technicians to prepare the ECG reports in the CAPA process.

A Form 483 is issued when the agency feels it has observed conditions that "may constitute violations of the Food Drug and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act and related Acts", so while the CEO says the company continues to "work diligently and collaboratively to resolve the warning letter to the FDA's satisfaction", but this is likely another headache that iRhythm did not need, that may impact its share price negatively today and in future, depending on how quickly a resolution is found.

Furthermore, as reported in the latest 10Q:

On April 4, 2023, we received a Subpoena Duces Tecum from the Consumer Protection Branch, Civil Division of the U.S. Department of Justice (the "DOJ", requesting production of various documents regarding our products and services. We are cooperating fully on these matters. On July 1, 2024, the DOJ filed with the United States District Court for the Northern District of California a Petition for Order to Show Cause and Application for Enforcement with respect to the production of certain documentary materials which we assert are protected by legal privileges. We intend to defend our privilege assertions over such materials in our response to the DOJ's petition.

Apparently, the DOJ was expected to respond to iRhythm on September 6th - I cannot find any information relating to this, but it is also worth noting that the FDA's warning letter apparently also accused iRhythm of failing to report patient deaths in a timely manner, labelling violations, and quality system violations.

As such, it seems that iRhythm is far from "out of the woods" in terms of the various investigations ongoing, requests for data, and various violations.

Looking Ahead - Tricky Outlook In A Challenging Market

Back in 2021, I covered iRhythm in a note for Seeking Alpha, shortly after the company's stock had plunged by 75%, from ~$250, to ~$75, after the amount of reimbursement it was eligible to receive from its Medicare clients was cut by two-thirds - from ~$300, to ~$100, by the Medicare Administrative Contractor [MAC] for south-central states, Novitas. As I wrote at the time:

Although the company derives only ~27% of its revenues from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services [CMS], the greater fear is that its private contractors will take note of Novitas' decision and adjust their own pricing strategies accordingly, dishing out another devastating blow to iRhythm.

I gave iRhythm a "buy" rating on the basis that the reimbursement decision may be reversed, which is ultimately what happened, sending the stock on a bull run from ~$45 per share, to ~$160 per share by September 2022.

Reimbursement - and its withdrawal if iRhythm does not fully comply with regulatory requirements - is a constant ongoing worry for the company, not only because of its significant exposure to Medicaid/Medicare, but also because if the government pays less, or not at all, for iRhythm's products and services, commercial payors will very likely follow suit.

For example, iRhythm warns in its latest 10Q:

Because remote cardiac monitoring technology, including the Zio System, is rapidly evolving, there is a continuing risk that relative value units assigned, and reimbursement rates set, by CMS may not adequately reflect the value and expense of this technology and associated monitoring services, and CMS may reduce these rates in the future, which would adversely affect our financial results.

Concluding Thoughts - There Is A Continuing Contrarian Trade In Play, But Headwinds Are Significant

To summarize today's news on iRhythm and how it may affect the company's prospects for share price growth going forward, the 510(k) approval was a significant win for the company, but it seems there are many more hurdles than need to be overcome before the company fully settles its issues with the FDA and DOJ.

There seems to be no guarantee at this time that all of the various problems will be resolved satisfactorily, and the reputational - not to mention the financial - damage the company may suffer is very significant.

Of course, we can speculate that should the issues be resolved, the threat of reimbursement reductions is reduced, leading to a rise in the share price, perhaps even a return to former highs of >$250 per share.

Balanced against that, however, is current financial performance, I'd argue. According to the company's Q2 earnings press release:

iRhythm projects revenue for the full year 2024 to grow approximately 18% to 20% compared to prior year results, ranging from approximately $580 million to $590 million. Gross margin for the full year 2024 is expected to range from 68.5% to 69% and adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year 2024 is expected to range from approximately 3.5% to 4% of revenues.

Given the current market cap of $2.35bn, we are looking at a forward price to sales ratio of ~4x, and there is no P/E ratio to consider as it seems that there will be no profits in 2024. As such, I'd question if there is genuinely a contrarian trade opportunity in play, as even if all regulatory issues are resolved, the products themselves are not generating sufficient revenues to justify a much higher share price.

From my perspective then, I would not be tempted to back iRhythm at this time as I believe firstly, that regulatory issues are not yet resolved fully, and secondly, the product itself is not generating sufficient revenues (I am not convinced we will see private of public reimbursement for products increase significantly).

Others may disagree, but I'm remaining on the sideline with regard to iRhythm. Shares are up >20% since my "Buy" call in 2021 - and at one point they were up >160%, so contrarians may still find reasons for optimism, but if the product itself is the real underlying issue, iRhythm could potentially resolve all of its outstanding issues and still remain unprofitable.

data.title: QQQY: A Closer Look Reveals A Flawed Income Strategy
data.description:
Investment Summary

The Defiance Nasdaq 100 Enhanced Options & 0DTE Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQY) is a recently launched ETF that uses a short-put strategy on the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ). The fund generates income for its investors by selling put options and distributes this income regularly. The current distribution rate is 42%, which seems quite high for the option strategy they apply. The fund charges an expense ratio of 0.99%, which is reasonable given the active management of the fund.

QQQY, like other options-based ETFs, aims to profit from options trading. These funds use various strategies, including selling puts and writing covered calls. The effectiveness of these strategies is influenced by factors such as expiration timing, trading frequency, strike price selection, and the interplay of call and put positions.

While it boasts a hefty distribution rate, a closer look reveals this figure might not be what it seems. There are concerns about the sustainability of such a high payout, with some suggesting that it may be partially supported by returning investors' capital to investors.

This article will analyze the current performance of QQQY and the historical distribution yield. We will then validate whether QQQY is a suitable investment alternative.

Fund Characteristics

The fund has been around for a little more than a year and currently manages $200 million in assets. This makes it a relatively new and small ETF, so we need to be cautious when we evaluate the recent stock performance.

The ETF has the following target:

QQQY aims to achieve consistent weekly yield distributions for investors coupled with equity market exposure to the Nasdaq-100. QQQY is an actively managed exchange-traded fund [ETF] that seeks enhanced income, constructed of treasuries and Nasdaq-100 index options. The strategy’s objective is to generate weekly distributions by selling option premium on a daily basis. The fund uses daily options to realize rapid time decay by selling in the money puts.

The Fund’s primary investment objective is to seek current income. The Fund’s secondary investment objective is to seek exposure to the performance of the Nasdaq 100 Index (the “Index”) subject to a limit on potential investment gains.

We want to highlight this strategy is completely different from a standard investment in shares of a Nasdaq ETF. QQQY will trade options on the Nasdaq Index and aims to generate income from these options trades, regardless of the price movement of the Nasdaq Index.

The put-write strategy they refer to involves selling put options on the Nasdaq 100 Index with very short maturities. The selling of the put options will generate investment income in the form of option premiums, which are then distributed to their investors weekly. This income will depend on the underlying volatility of the Nasdaq and the total return will not be comparable to that of the Nasdaq. Unlike a long position in the Nasdaq, short-put positions have capped upside potential. In exchange for this limited upside, the seller of the put option receives a premium.

From the above chart, we can see an investment in QQQY would have yielded a total return of roughly 20% in the past year, whereas a similar investment in QQQ would have yielded a 37% ROI, over the same 1-year period. This outperformance of the Nasdaq should not come as a surprise, as the Nasdaq has unlimited upside potential, which paid off in the past year thanks to the strong bull market in tech stocks.

While the 20% ROI for QQQY is relatively strong, it does seem fairly low compared with the fund's current distribution rates, which are 2x as high.

Strategy Evaluation

QQQY applies a short-dated put-write strategy on the Nasdaq Index. Selling puts near-expiration benefits from time decay, which reduces option value. As QQQY sells the put options, they want their option value to decay as much as possible. The problem here is that the sold options have such a short maturity profile, that there remains little premium left to erode.

Selling puts can generate income, but it comes at a cost. The fund's potential for significant gains is limited. If the stock price surges, the fund won't fully capitalize on the increase. The maximum gain that can be obtained by selling a put option is the amount of premium that the seller receives once the put option is sold. In the case of QQQY, this premium is bound to be rather limited as the put options have a short lifespan, which limits the potential premium amount of the sold put options.

In essence, the fund is giving up the potential upside for immediate income. This strategy can be effective in certain market conditions, but it's important to understand the trade-offs. While it offers a steady stream of income, it limits the fund's ability to participate fully in upward market movements.

The income from these put option trades is closely tied to the Nasdaq's implied volatility. Higher volatility means greater uncertainty about the future stock price, which leads to higher option premiums. This, in turn, benefits QQQY by increasing the income it can generate from its short option trades.

From the above chart, we can see that the implied volatility of the Nasdaq has been fluctuating around the 20% level in the past year. This is a reasonable level of volatility, not extremely high, but still sufficiently high to make selling put options a viable strategy.

Let's have a look at the current holdings of QQQY. From the below table, we can see only a very small portion of the fund is allocated to a short put option position (0.34%).

We cannot expect the fund to have a position of 60% in short put option positions (due to margin requirements), but the current allocation seems small on a portfolio level. The majority of the assets in invested in short-term US Treasuries and cash.

Given the fund's limited investment in put option trades, it raises questions about how it can consistently generate such high annualized yields every week. It's important to remember that short-put options have limited profit potential due to their capped upside and short lifespan. If the fund only allocates 0.34% of its total funds to these trades with limited profit potential, how can it obtain annualized distribution yields of +40% to its investors? This matter is addressed in the next section.

Distribution Composition

To understand how the fund pays out a distribution of 30%-40%, we need to look at the components of a distribution payment. There are 2 components here:

  • Net Investment Income: This is the investment income from the option trades.
  • Return of Capital: This part of the distribution represents a part of the investor's initial investment and is distinct from the income or capital gains generated by the investment.

The second component ("return of capital") is simply a part of the initial investment being returned to investors. The fund did not gain this money by performing option trades, it's just a part of the initial investment they include in their calculation of the total distribution yield.

For the distribution of October 11th, the "return of capital" part represents 2/3 of the total distribution. Only 1/3 of the distribution amount came from the investment income of the option trades. By using this tactic, it's easy to promote elevated distribution yields to your investors. If the fund would only apply the investment income as the sole input in the distribution yields, the total yields would "only" be 10%-15%.

The price performance of QQQY demonstrates the detrimental impact of systematically returning part of the initial investment back to the shareholders:

The share price of QQQY declined week after week, which is a direct consequence of the "return of capital" strategy.

To assess the fund's performance, investors should focus on its total return, which combines price appreciation and distributions. While the advertised 40% distribution yield might be tempting, the total return is the true indicator of whether QQQY is a suitable investment.

Due to its distribution feature, QQQY's price is expected to decline over time, while maintaining a high monthly distribution rate. Given its close correlation with the Nasdaq, a severe market downturn could result in significant losses for QQQY. Conversely, in a bull market, QQQY's performance is anticipated to be roughly half of the Nasdaq's gains, influenced by the speed of the uptrend and the prevailing market volatility.

Due to these reasons, we would not recommend an investment in QQQY, certainly not as an alternative to investing directly in the Nasdaq Index.

Conclusion

QQQY's high yield might seem tempting, but its strategy has some serious flaws. Short-term puts limit its upside, and low-yield Treasuries drag down its performance. The advertised yield is inflated as they include the "return of capital" in the total distribution yield calculation. Don't be fooled by the high yield; focus on the total return.

In conclusion, QQQY might not be the best choice for investors looking to capitalize on the Nasdaq's growth potential. The high yield comes at the cost of limited upside and a heavy investment in conservative assets. Think carefully before investing.

data.title: Zenas Bio: Recent IPO With Late-Stage Candidate
data.description:
Recently IPO-ed Zenas BioPharma, Inc. (NASDAQ:ZBIO) is developing obexelimab, a B-cell therapeutic targeting CD19 and FcγRIIb. The molecule was acquired from Xencor (XNCR) in 2021 against an equity stake. Target indications are multiple autoimmune diseases, including IgG4-related disease (IgG4-RD), multiple sclerosis (MS), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (wAIHA). Obexelimab is in a phase 3 trial called Indigo targeting IgG4-RD.

Mechanism of action and positioning

Obexelimab is a CD19 directed B-cell modulating, not depleting, therapy for indications where overactive and overexpressed B-cells make the body's immune system attack the body itself. The therapeutic idea here is to partially deactivate but not completely deplete B-cells when they become pathologic. Once their pathologic activity subsides, therapy should be able to recover them quickly so that B-cells can perform their natural action - which is to protect the body from outside infection after differentiating into plasma cells.

The molecule also targets the FcγRIIb receptor, which reduces the activation of B-cells, leading to a downregulation of the immune response. The overall effect is intended to modulate B-cell overactivity while preserving essential immune functions, an aim that is intended to reduce side effects.

Targeting CD19 and FcγRIIb enables Obexelimab to target more of the B-cell lineage, including pro-B cells, pre-B cells, B cells, plasmablasts, and select plasma cells, each of which has specific pathologic effect in specific diseases.

The dual inhibition also inhibits but not fully depletes B-cells, which can potentially make it safer than B-cell depleting therapies. Also to be noted that following treatment, B-cell recovery has been observed within weeks, not months, unlike these therapies. The subcutaneous administration is convenient and can be done at home.

Lead indication

IgG4-related disease (IgG4-RD) is a rare disease; it is a chronic inflammatory condition where IgG4-positive plasma cells infiltrate normal tissue, leading to fibrosis and organ malfunction. IgG4-RD can affect any organ, but more commonly appears in the pancreas, bile ducts, salivary glands, kidneys, and lymph nodes.

There are no approved therapies; corticosteroids, immunosuppressive drugs and so on are used. The disease is highly undiagnosed, but it is estimated that there are 1500 patients in the US alone. The company says there are 20,000 patients diagnosed with the disease in the USA. The company also says that "IgG4-RD is projected to be a ~$3 billion commercial opportunity in the U.S. alone."

Obexelimab data

In August 2023, exactly a year before its IPO, Zenas announced data from the phase 2 trial of obexelimab in IgG4-RD. Data was published in Lancet. The study "found obexelimab produced rapid, strong, and sustained clinical improvement, including complete clinical remission, in most patients with active IgG4-RD." This was an open label, single arm study - recall that there are no approved treatments for the disease, and glucocorticoids commonly used to treat it come with significant side effects and immune impairment. The data for the study were:

At baseline, 12 (80%) of 15 patients had an elevated median serum IgG4 concentration of 220 mg/dL (IQR 124-441), and the median IgG4-related disease responder index score was 12 (IQR 7-13). 12 (80%) of 15 patients achieved the primary endpoint (i.e., primary responders), 14 (93%) were defined as responders. Reductions from baseline in serum B cells and plasmablasts were observed following treatment with obexelimab. However, in most patients with follow-up data, serum B cells recovered to 75% of baseline concentrations within 42 days of the final obexelimab dose. 13 (87%) of 15 patients reported adverse events, one of which (an infusion reaction) resulted in treatment discontinuation.

The CR rate was very high, and the recovery rate and duration of B-cells was also satisfactory. The safety profile, too, was pretty decent, with mild and manageable adverse events.

Financials

The market cap of ZBIO is $970mn and cash balance was $184mn as of the last quarter. In May, the company announced a $200mn Series C financing from "Enavate Sciences, Longitude Capital, and other new and existing investors." In September, the company also went public with a $225mn IPO.

Because this is a new IPO, I don't have adequate information about their operating expenses, including R&D and G&A expenses. However, last year's data as I see here is $17mn for the whole year. They also have a lot of cash right now, so I believe they have a pretty decent cash runway for such a late-stage company. Their phase 3 trial began in January 2023 and the actual study is for 52 weeks, so they should have topline data in the not too distant future (late 2025). As such, they should have enough cash runway to last that long, at the least.

The company has a collaboration agreement with Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) to develop and commercialize obexelimab for autoimmune diseases in Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, Hong Kong and Australia. BMS paid them $50mn in upfront fees for the collab.

Risks

ZBIO is a recent IPO and I normally do not invest in them because of the uncertainty around IPOs, lock up period expiration and the resulting change in the stock price, and so on.

The other unknown here is that, being a recent IPO, there is not enough data - for example, financial data - to assess Zenas BioPharma, Inc. properly.

Bottom line

It is interesting to see a recent IPO with such a late-stage asset in an area of unmet need. I will continue watching Zenas BioPharma, Inc. stock with interest as it moves away from the IPO, and will see about investing when it stabilizes.

data.title: Hercules Capital: The Valuation Is My Only Concern
data.description:
Hercules Capital, Inc. (NYSE:HTGC) has gained a well-deserved reputation in the investment community as a higher quality business development company, or BDC. It offers a highly attractive forward dividend yield of just under 10% which is also supported by a strong net investment income coverage ratio. However, despite these positive factors, the stock's premium to net asset value appears too high, in my view, given the current economic outlook and move towards a lower rate environment.

The portfolio

At the time of my previous article, I noted that there appeared to be early indications of improving portfolio quality at HTGC. This observation was made in light of the decline (improvement) of the portfolio weighted average risk rating based on management’s internal risk rating system. However, in its most recent earnings report, there was a slight increase in the weighted average risk rating (deterioration) from the weighted average risk rating of 2.16 in the first quarter to 2.18 in the second quarter.

This seems to have been driven by an increase in the percentage of the BDCs portfolio risk graded at Grade 3. The percentage of the portfolio risk graded at Grade 3 increased to 31% of the portfolio at cost, compared to around 29.2% in the first quarter. HTGC uses a debt investment grading system, ranging from 1 to 5, with 1 representing the highest quality, to evaluate and monitor the anticipated risk levels of debt investments within its portfolio. The grade assigned to a portfolio company can fluctuate depending on its performance and the timing of expected liquidity events. A downgrade may occur if a company does not meet financing requirements, falls short of its business targets, or approaches a point where additional equity is necessary to continue operations. On the other hand, an upgrade might be given if the company exceeds financial expectations or is approaching full repayment or maturity due to a liquidity event.

While the increase in Grade 3 may lead to a slight deterioration in the overall risk rating of the portfolio, it does not give rise to substantial concern at this time. The majority of the portfolio continues to be rated at the higher end of the internal risk rating system, with around two-thirds of the portfolio rated at the lower risk Grades of Grade 1 and 2 as depicted in the chart above. Therefore, in my view, migration between the different risk categories remains something that investors should consider, but it is not a decisive factor in evaluating HTGC at this point.

This is further supported by the relatively low percentage of the portfolio on non-accrual status. In this respect, management noted that –

In Q2, the number of loans on non-accrual remained the same, with the write-off of one loan and the addition of a new one. We had two debt investments on non-accrual with an investment cost and fair value of approximately $91.8 million and $32.1 million, respectively, or 2.5% and 0.9% as a percentage of the company's total investment portfolio at cost and value, respectively.”

This is particularly noteworthy, given that an uptick in non-accruals in BDCs had long been expected. In my view, HTGC has a solid history of careful portfolio risk management that serves its investors well. Fitch similarly observed that “Hercules' asset quality has been strong. Net realized losses as a percentage of the total portfolio, at value, averaged 0.2% annually from 2020 through 2023, which compares favorably with peers.”

Net investment income and the dividend

HTGC has long benefited from rising interest rates, given that around 97% of its portfolio is composed of floating rate investments. With interest rates now dropping somewhat, some of these benefits are likely to partially reversed and could see a decline in net investment income [NII]. Nevertheless, management recently observed that “As a reminder, more than 97% of our portfolio is floating with a floor, so any Fed decreases in interest rate may not have an equal reduction in our core yield.”

According to BDC Universe, the average interest rate floor at HTGC is around 5.18%. This interest rate floor represents the minimum interest rate that a borrower from HTGC would pay regardless of any drops in interest rates. These interest rate floors will protect HTGC’s NII to a degree should interest rates drop substantially. Nevertheless, a decrease in interest rates is still likely to have a negative impact on NII as the BDCs core yield is much higher than the average interest rate floor.

Despite these challenges, HTGC’s dividend is well covered by NII and unlikely to be reduced simply because of a slight decrease in NII associated with lower rates. In the second quarter, HTGC generated NII per share of around $0.51 per share, which is well above the ordinary distribution of $0.4 per share. This provides the BDC with a NII coverage ratio of around 128% for the second quarter.

HTGC’s one-year average NII coverage ratio is also just over 128% indicating a well-covered dividend. Its one-year average NII coverage ratio is higher than that of some of its peers such as Ares Capital (ARCC) albeit substantially lower than that of other peers such as Main Street Capital (MAIN). Nevertheless, the high dividend coverage ratio makes a cut to the ordinary distribution highly unlikely.

Valuation

At the time of my previous article, I observed that the premium for HTGC appears to be too high in the current environment. While there was a dip in HTGC's share price in August, the share price has largely recovered from this dip and is essentially right back at the higher premium to net asset value [NAV] that I had previously expressed concern over.

HTGC is now trading at a premium to NAV of just under 77%, which is the second highest of any BDC. A premium to peers is certainly justified given its long history of excellent performance. However, it is much higher than HTGC's 3-year average premium to NAV of around 48%. In my view, it is difficult to justify a substantial premium at a time when some of the factors that have contributed to a substantial rise in NII, i.e., high-interest rates, will play a diminished role going forward.

Conclusion

While Hercules Capital demonstrates solid portfolio management, evidenced by its relatively low non-accrual rates and strong dividend coverage, the primary concern lies in HTGC's valuation. Trading at a significant premium of 77% to NAV, well above its historical average, the current premium appears excessive given the potential decline in net investment income as interest rates drop. Therefore, despite its solid fundamentals, I rate HTGC a hold due to its high premium relative to peers and its historical average.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Continued Equity Divestitures Signal Uncertainty
data.description:
BRK stock kept trimming its equity portfolio

My last work on Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) was motivated by a significant insider transaction, as suggested in the title of that article “Berkshire Hathaway: Ajit Jain Halved Position, Time To Reexamine Stock”. More specifically, I explained that:

New developments surrounding BRK have led me to reexamine my early buy rating. A top development on my list is Ajit Jain's divestiture. It has led me to reexamine the valuation risks under current conditions and my assumption of BRK’s book value growth potential. These updated assessments show me that the valuation risks are higher and the growth potential lower than I thought.

Since that writing, I think a key development surrounding BRK that investors now need to consider is the continued trimming of its equity portfolio. I assume BRK investors are all aware that the company has reduced its exposure to Apple (AAPL) substantially in its August disclosure (see the next chart below).

Since that disclosure, BRK has also kept divesting its Bank of America (BAC) position aggressively. More specifically (the emphases were added by me),

Barrons report (October 18, 2024): Berkshire began unloading Bank of America stock recently and now has sold more than 260 million shares for over $10 billion at an average price of around $41 a share. Berkshire Hathaway sold more Bank of America stock on Tuesday after the bank reported third-quarter earnings, bringing its stake to just below 10%.

As BRK’s stake in BAC now is under 10%, it is no longer required to disclose its further transactions frequently. Thus, even if there is further trimming, investors now have to wait till the end of next quarterly earnings to find out.

Against this backdrop, the remainder of this article is to explain why the disclosed divestitures thus far are already sufficient to cause concerns for me regarding BRK’s further capital allocation and return potential. I will show that the liquidity added by these trimmings so far could A) either mean a lack of attractive capital deployment opportunities going forward or B) another elephant gun in preparation. For scenario B, I will elaborate on the potential for BRK to acquire Chubb Limited (CB) in particular. Given such uncertainties, I consider the stock a HOLD under current conditions.

BRK stock’s cash problem

The screenshot here provides a breakdown of its total cash and short-term investments in the past 10 years up to the latest report (filed in early August). As seen, BRK's total cash position has fluctuated a bit from time to time, but the overall trend is a rapid upward trend. A decade ago, in 2014, the total stood at about $60 billion. These figures more than doubled to $167 billion by 2023.

Historically, having too much cash has been a problem – a good one and also a well-known one – for BRK, as you can tell from the above figures. It's just I consider the problem to be especially pronounced now to cause concerns about its future capital deployment opportunities. As seen, since the end of 2023, its cash position surged to almost $277 billion in the latest report (again that was filed in early August). The trimming of BAC position since then added another $8 to $10 billion to its cash position, based on my estimates. Together with its organic earnings and dividend incomes during this period, its cash position as of this writing could be close to $300 billion, close to 30% of its total market cap (of $1 trillion as of this writing).

In recent years, the return from its equity portfolio, especially its top positions like AAPL and BAC, has been a key driver for BRK’s total return (which has been terrific by the way as seen in the next chart below). As an example, Berkshire Hathaway has achieved a total return of 119.5% in the past 5 years, surpassing the broader market represented by the SPY ETF, which has returned 110.3%. A key performance driver was AAPL, which significantly outperformed both BRK and SPY with a total return of 306.4%. Note that the BAC return of 52.44% during this period severely understated BRK’s gain. BRK’s BAC shares were accumulated under far better entry points and also under special deals.

Now, with close to 30% of its market cap sitting on its ledger as cash and the overall market trading at an elevated valuation, I am very uncertain if the company can find good opportunities to deploy this record amount of capital in the near future. Bonds are not a bad idea with treasury rates hovering between 4% and 5% but are unlikely to lead to outperformance.

Given its current trillion-dollar size, the company needs an elephant-sized opportunity, and I think there is a potential one available, as detailed next.

BRK stock: potential acquisition of CB

I see several reasons and indicators that suggest a full acquisition of CB as a possible scenario. First and foremost, BRK has been aggressively adding CB shares in recent quarters. As you can see from the chart below, as the company divests its AAPL and BAC positions substantially, it has been building up its CB position quickly. In Q3 2023, BRK started a relatively small position with about 8.1 million CB shares, translating into only about 0.54% of its overall equity portfolio size. This was only to be followed by a series of significant additions. As of the latest disclosure, BRK has acquired more than 27 million CB shares as seen, making CB the 9th top position in BRK’s portfolio worth about $6.89 billion.

Fundamentally, CB would be a good fit into the BRK empire in my view. As argued in an earlier analysis on CB,

Buffett has displayed his fondness for insurance companies on numerous occasions. He has called Berkshire’s P/C (property/casualty) insurance segment the core of the BRK conglomerate he’s built over the years. And his actions are certainly well aligned with his words. He has been buying a range of sizable insurance companies outright over the years, including National Indemnity, GEICO, Gen Re, and most recently Alleghany in 2022. Adding a gem (or another gem) such as CB to its mix of wholly owned subsidiaries seems to be a very logical move considering this pattern, in my view.

And affordability is not a concern at all. CB’s current market cap is about $115 B as of this writing and as noted above, BRK already owns close to $7B of its shares. With BRK’s current cash pile (again, close to $300 B by my estimate), the acquisition could be easily financed by an all-cash deal.

Other risks and final thoughts

Besides the difficulty of finding an elephant-sized investment opportunity, another downside risk in my mind is the current valuation risks embedded in the stock. To wit, the table below compares the valuation ratios of BRK to the sector median and its own 5-year average. The overall picture I see indicates sizable overvaluation risks. BRK is currently trading at a substantial premium relative to both its peers and its own historical valuation. As an example, its TTM P/E ratio is about 23.86 as seen, which is almost 2x higher than the sector median and about 20% above its own 5-year average. Additionally, the price-to-book ratio shows the same trend. The P/B ratio for BRK.B is about 1.51x, about 24% higher than the sector median and about 13% above its own 5-year average.

To be crystally clear and also to conclude, I am not trying to argue that BRK is a bad investment. There is nothing wrong with owning a wonderful business like BRK even when valuation shows some premium. My point is, as suggested in the title, that I see uncertainties in its return potential going forward given its continued equity divestitures in recent quarters. Such divestitures could indicate a lack of opportunities for capital development given the current elevated market valuation, although there are some potential elephant-sized opportunities and a full buyout of CB is a possible scenario. But I doubt, even if the buyout actually materializes, its return can match those BRK has enjoyed with its AAPL or BAC investments in recent years.

data.title: Viasat: Best To Stay On The Sidelines
data.description:
Introduction

Viasat, Inc. (NASDAQ:VSAT), a small-cap proxy on the global communication technology and services market, has experienced a disastrous 12 months. Granted, it's not as if other small-cap technology stocks have dazzled either, but at least, they've managed to stay afloat, with almost a flattish return profile over a 1-year basis; conversely, VSAT has been taken to the cleaners, losing over 60% of its value.

With the stock now not far from dropping to single-digit levels (the last time the stock floundered at such low levels was over two decades ago, in 2003), is there some salvation to be found at these beaten-down levels, or could we see more of the same?

Still Plenty Of Concerns

VSAT offers a plethora of connectivity solutions, but it currently reports under two broad segments- Communication Services, and Defense and Advanced Technologies. Of the two, the latter takes greater precedence as it contributes 77% of total sales, and 83% of total EBITDA, and it doesn't help when your main contributor isn't growing.

For the uninitiated, VSAT offers broadband and narrowband services for markets such as government, aviation, residential, maritime, energy, etc. In Q1, note that revenue for this segment had dropped by 2% (on a combined basis), driven in large part by weakness seen in the fixed services and other segment (accounted for the largest share of communication services revenue last year at 30%) which is the proxy for fixed broadband services for residential and business clients.

This is not a one-off, and for the rest of the year, VSAT expects US fixed broadband revenue to remain challenged (mainly on account of weak residential subscription, driven in part by Starlink's entry), and play a part in ensuring that revenue for the communication services sees a decline for the whole year.

Within Communication Services, one of the other businesses that appears to be faring rather well (19% growth in Q1) is the aviation services business, which also contributes 30% of the segment revenue. But, this is a segment that is expected to see plenty of sequential volatility, and with the latest reports pointing to how commercial airline delivery delays have not improved, investors would do well not to get too euphoric. Worryingly, investors should also be concerned by the growing trend of airlines moving their in-flight Wi-Fi to Starlink's laser-linked satellites. VSAT has of late been winning a healthy chunk of IFS (In-flight service) contracts, but one does wonder if this will start ebbing. It's worth noting that VSAT's order backlog has already been on a declining trend for a while now.

Then, for a business with close to $7.5bn in gross debt, and around $400-$450m of interest costs p.a, it is quite troubling to note that VSAT does not generate positive EBIT on an FY basis.

Meanwhile, over the past decade, VSAT's operating cash flow has never been meaningful enough to cover either its CAPEX commitments or its debt burden. The net leverage in Q1 may have dipped to 3.5x, but management expects this to increase from current levels, and end higher by the end of FY25.

On the CAPEX front, we admit that the growth component should start easing as more of VSAT's high-throughput satellites go into operation (ViaSat-3F1 went live in August, and ViaSat-3F2 and ViaSat-3F3 are expected to go live in mid to late 2025), but even the FY26 guide calls for CAPEX commitments to the tune of $1.2bn, which certainly isn't a pittance.

Prima facie, at a forward EV/EBITDA of just 4.3x, VSAT may come across as very cheap, more so as it translates to a rough 40% discount over its long-term average of around 7x.

However, dig a little deeper, and you'll begin to wonder if you're getting good enough EBITDA growth for such a low multiple. Firstly, don't get carried away by the 16% EBITDA growth seen in Q1-25, as this pace will likely slow as we progress through the rest of the year; indeed, management has only guided to a mid-single-digit YoY increase in EBITDA growth for the whole of FY25, which would imply a much slower pace of growth for the rest of the year.

Looking beyond next year, and through FY27, it does not appear that VSAT offers compelling EBITDA growth either. In fact, taking last year's adjusted EBITDA of $1.488bn as a base, and looking at consensus numbers for the next three years, it appears we have a business that is poised only to generate a 3-year CAGR of 3% (for context, over the past three years, VSAT's historical 3-year CAGR has been a lot better at over 12%), which is not a great threshold of growth for a business priced at 4x EBITDA.

Investors may also want to consider that VSAT has a history of seeing significant EBITDA revisions; for context, note that next year's EBITDA numbers have already been trimmed by 7% over the last six months, so don't rule out further contractions, given the headwinds in some of their markets.

Closing Thoughts - What Do The Charts Suggest?

It's also rather clear that large swathes of the market, don't have a favorable perspective on VSAT's prospects now, and this is reflected in the charts. If you want to bet against the trend, good luck to you!

On the daily chart, bearish conditions can be reiterated by the price trading well below its key moving averages - the 50-day MA, 100-day MA, and 200-day MA.

This will embolden the bears, who have once again started boosting their short positions. Note that the percentage of float that is short had been on a slightly declining trend through August, but has once again started trending up since September and currently stands at 16%.

Meanwhile, one may also be discouraged to note that the smart money also appears to be bailing from this counter after they had shown some encouraging signs of buying the dip in June and July. For context, the net shares owned by institutions are now down by over 14% over the past three months!

To get a bird's eye view of VSAT's price action, one may also look at VSAT's monthly chart, and here we can see that bearish momentum is still in full flow, with a series of lower-lows (LLs), and lower-highs (LHs). We've also seen the price break down from strong support in 2003 and 2008, and despite some tenuous attempts to recoup that zone, we didn't get sufficient positive momentum.

Until we see some abatement in the series of LLs and LHs and a flattening out of the price action, we don't think investors should take the plunge, as the RSI is yet to hit the oversold zone, and the price is also not trading below its lower Bollinger band (which basically implies that the selling is far from overdone).

data.title: SOXL: Playing With Fire
data.description:
It's been nearly impossible to lose money in the semiconductor space over the last couple of years. Since the end of 2021, the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) has produced a massive 51% total return against a broad market that has been closer to 34% — which is still arguably terrific, just not as terrific as semis.

There can be little doubt the entire semiconductor space has benefited from the AI-driven excitement made possible by companies like Nvidia (NVDA). To the chagrin of some, myself included, NVDA continues to prove doubters wrong and make new highs. However, things have started to favor broad market equities over the semiconductor ETF since July. The SOXX chart doesn't look all that great in my view:

For those who practice technical analysis, it appears as though we have a head and shoulders pattern forming on the SOXX chart. This is typically viewed as bearish. In my view, this trend in SOXX is something that everyone playing in the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:SOXL) should be paying attention to.

Fund Details and Performance

I've covered leveraged ETFs for Seeking Alpha a handful of times. My general take has typically been that leveraged funds are terrible long-term investment products that should be avoided entirely by most traders and investors. SOXL is a fund that tracks many of the same positions that can be observed in SOXX. But it does so with 3x leverage and a daily re-balancing strategy that should ultimately lead to under-performance of the fund. This hasn't stopped SOXL shares from amassing a sizable $11.2 billion AUM since launching over a decade ago:

Fund Details SOXL
Expense Ratio 0.76%
Yield 0.88%
Assets Under Management $11.22b
Inception 3/11/2010

Source: Seeking Alpha.

As with any leveraged ETF, it's important to view SOXL against an un-leveraged semiconductor comp over time, so investors can get a sense for what happens if they don't make entries and exits appropriately. SOXL ripped a nearly 400% gain in less than a year if we start at the end of October 2023:

However, since that time, SOXL has managed to keep just a 137% gain against a 56.3% gain in SOXX. So what's the problem? What if instead of buying the bottom, we bought the top of the semis in mid-July via SOXL?

In that instance, we're down over 43% against an un-leveraged semiconductor ETF that has corrected closer to 10-20%. At present, the 3x leveraged ETF has actually produced 4x the losses if we measure from the top of the July rally. Of course, it's perhaps less fair to judge these things from tops and bottoms since buyers and sellers will almost always fail to execute with such precision. But even if we observe through just the beginning of the year, we see the leveraged fund not holding up over time:

As shown, year to date, SOXX is up just under 20% while SOXL is up only 12.3%. Meaning, not only is SOXL not producing a 3x semiconductor return so far this year, but holding it over this length of time would produce a result that lags the broader market. I think the point is clear, SOXL can move dramatically higher when semis are crushing it with consistency. But buyers of the fund shares have to really get the timing right and can't be afraid to lock in wins when they have them. Thus, the fundamental setup for the companies that SOXL tracks are important. Is it time to be jumping into SOXL given the pullback in the semi space?

Top Ten Holdings & Insider Moves

The top ten holdings in SOXL account for 58% of the total fund capital. If it is profitability you seek, I don't know that we can ask much more from the top ten companies in the semi space. Almost all of these tickers have an A+ profitability grade from Seeking Alpha:

I've sorted those names by Seeking Alpha Quant Score in the picture above. Despite the terrific profitability grades, only two of the ten have a Quant Score over 4. Furthermore, six of the ten stocks have a valuation grade of D+ or worse. Of course, companies can stay overvalued (or overbought) for a very long time. Without a clear catalyst that triggers a revaluation, overvalued isn't necessarily a sell indication all on its own. Companies can certainly grow into valuations over time.

However, I think we can often get signals from what insiders are doing. And the Q3 insider sales story isn't all that encouraging:

Top Ten Holdings Q3 Insider Sales Q4 Insider Sales
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) $27.3 -
Broadcom Limited (AVGO) $51.3 $22.0
Nvidia $930.7 $40.7
Qualcomm (QCOM) $6.7 $2.4
Texas Instruments (TXN) $17.7 -
Micron Technology (MU) $1.2 -
Intel (INTC) Net buyers -
Applied Materials (AMAT) - -
Lam Research (LRCX) $38.1 $0.5
Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) $95.0 $12.5

Source: OpenInsider, values in millions.

Looking at each of these tickers individually, insider buying is nearly non-existent, while insider selling remains an ongoing story. Of the ten stocks listed above, only Intel had insider buying in the 3rd quarter. Applied Materials didn't have any insider sales in Q3, but that company did endure about $114 million of insider sales in June alone. For the third quarter, there was over $1.1 billion of insider sales in total for all of these companies.

Now, I'd be remiss if I didn't mention that the tickers above generally have multi-billion valuations. Thus, the insider selling may not be concerning to every investor. Furthermore, most of the $1.1 billion sale total is due to Jensen Huang selling what amounts to a small fraction of his NVDA holdings. But the larger point that I'm trying to convey here is leveraged ETFs do better when there is a clear-sustained trend higher. I'm not sure if we can make the case that SOXL has that given the technicals, valuations, and insider activity.

Final Takeaways

Leveraged ETFs are financial products that should exclusively be used by skilled traders with experience. Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in this product. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

This is especially true for 3x leveraged ETFs like SOXL. Daily rebalancing of leveraged funds historically leads to long-term decay and underperformance against the underlying assets that these types of funds track. SOXL is no different. But as I view it, the risk in going long SOXL today is heightened for a few reasons; technically, the semis look bad. Fundamentally, they're overvalued. And while overvaluation isn't necessarily an immediate reason to sell, insiders of almost every single Top 10 SOXL stock certainly have been.

The time to buy SOXL was October 2023. 9 months later, the fund had returned roughly 400%. It's been roughly cut in half since, even while NVDA is still at an all-time high. Things can, and probably will, get much worse for SOXL traders who hold shares too long. I'd avoid it.
